request.question: Name one of Bob Dylan's most-performed live songs, that is said to be inspired by the Prospect Park Water Tower in Minneapolis.

title: Kenwood Park Water Tower
Park Slope
Butler Square
Harry W. Jones House
Washburn Park Water Tower
All Along the Watchtower
Highland Park Tower
Prospect Park, Minneapolis
Prospect Park Water Tower
Old Chicago Water Tower District
sentences: The Kenwood Park Water Tower is an octagonal brick and stone water tower in the Kenwood neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.
It was built in 1910 and designed by Frederick William Cappelen, the city of Minneapolis engineer at the time.
The tower is 110 feet tall, making it the tallest structure in Kenwood.
The tower has not been used to store water since 1954.
The tower was built to alleviate water pressure and storage problems in the Lowry Hill area.
Although the tower is not the work of a master architect or representative of a specific architectural style, its design is distinctive.
It is ornamented with projecting ribs, narrow rectangular windows, and Lombard bands, suggesting a medieval fortress.
The tower serves as a distinct visual focus within the neighborhood.
Park Slope is a neighborhood in northwest Brooklyn, New York City.
Park Slope is roughly bounded by Prospect Park and Prospect Park West to the east, Fourth Avenue to the west, Flatbush Avenue to the north, and Prospect Expressway to the south.

Generally, the section from Flatbush Avenue to Garfield Place (the "named streets") is considered the "North Slope", the section from 1st through 9th Streets is considered the "Center Slope", and south of 10th Street, the "South Slope".
The neighborhood takes its name from its location on the western slope of neighboring Prospect Park.
Fifth Avenue and Seventh Avenue are its primary commercial streets, while its east-west side streets are lined with brownstones and apartment buildings.
Butler Square (originally the Butler Brothers Company building) is a former warehouse and office building in Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.
The building is located within the Minneapolis warehouse district and was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1971.
It is significant for its restrained Chicago School design by major Minneapolis architect Harry Wild Jones, and as a leading example of the older warehouse/office buildings in Minneapolis–Saint Paul.
Jones designed other buildings in Minneapolis such as the Minneapolis Scottish Rite Temple, Calvary Baptist Church, the Lakewood Cemetery Chapel, and the Washburn Park Water Tower.
The Harry W. Jones House is a historic house in Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States, in the Tangletown neighborhood.
Harry Wild Jones was one of the most notable architects in Minneapolis, with a career spanning nearly 50 years.
His designs included the Butler Brothers Warehouse, the chapel in Lakewood Cemetery, and the Washburn Park Water Tower.
His own home, built in 1887, was one of the first homes in the Washburn Park area and influenced the design of other homes in the area.
The house is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

The Washburn Park Water Tower poses as a landmark of early 20th-Century architectural achievement within the Tangletown neighborhood in south Minneapolis, Minnesota, and has been doing so for nearly 75 years.

Perched on top of one of the highest points in south Minneapolis, the tower is given the privilege to boast its unique location and role as an unofficial "beacon" for incoming planes landing at Minneapolis-Saint Paul International Airport, yet remains hidden from much of the residents and visitors that pass by the base of the hill each day.

This is mainly because large homes and tall oak trees scatter the hillside where the tower resides, and even more so, because of the clustered mess of streets and dead ends that compromise the towers' occupancy.
Hence the name, 'Tangletown'.
"All Along the Watchtower" is a song written and recorded by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan.
The song initially appeared on his 1967 album "John Wesley Harding", and it has been included on most of Dylan's subsequent greatest hits compilations.
Since the late 1970s, he has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs.
Different versions appear on four of Dylan's live albums.
The Highland Park Water Tower is a water tower in the Highland Park area of Saint Paul, Minnesota.
It was designed by Clarence W. Wigington, the nation's first African-American municipal architect.
The tower was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1986.
It was completed in 1928 at a cost of $69,483.
Prospect Park is a historic neighborhood within the University community of the U.S. city of Minneapolis, Minnesota.

The area is bounded by the Mississippi River to the south, the City of Saint Paul, Minnesota to the east, the Burlington Northern railroad yard to the north, and the Stadium Village commercial district of the University of Minnesota to the west.
The neighborhood is composed of several districts which include the East River Road area.
The 1913 Prospect Park Water Tower is a landmark and neighborhood icon.

The Prospect Park Water Tower, sometimes referred to as the "Witch's Hat Water Tower" or just "The Tower", is a historic water tower in the Prospect Park neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.
It was built in 1913 on Tower Hill Park, a hilltop park established in 1906.
The water tower has become the neighborhood's architectural mascot for its singular design by Frederick William Cappelen.
The tower is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song "All Along the Watchtower," as the tower was clearly visible from Dylan's home in nearby Dinkytown.
The Old Chicago Water Tower District is a historic district along the Magnificent Mile shopping district in the Near North Side community area of Chicago, Illinois.
The district is located on both sides of North Michigan Avenue between East Chicago and East Pearson Streets.
It includes the Chicago Water Tower, Chicago Avenue Pumping Station, and Chicago Fire Department Fire Station No. 98.
All three structures are part of the Chicago Landmark district designated on October 6, 1971 (amended June 10, 1981).
The Water Tower and Pumping Station were jointly added to the National Register of Historic Places on April 23, 1975.
In addition the Tower was named an American Water Landmark in 1969.
The Water Tower was also one of the few buildings to survive the Great Chicago Fire.
The district is the namesake of the nearby Water Tower Place.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Name one of Bob Dylan's most-performed live songs, that is said to be inspired by the Prospect Park Water Tower in Minneapolis.
hop1_docs: Prospect Park Water Tower | The Prospect Park Water Tower, sometimes referred to as the "Witch's Hat Water Tower" or just "The Tower", is a historic water tower in the Prospect Park neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.  It was built in 1913 on Tower Hill Park, a hilltop park established in 1906.  The water tower has become the neighborhood's architectural mascot for its singular design by Frederick William Cappelen.  The tower is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song "All Along the Watchtower," as the tower was clearly visible from Dylan's home in nearby Dinkytown.

As I Went Out One Morning | "As I Went Out One Morning" is a song written by Bob Dylan, released on his 1967 album "John Wesley Harding".  He has only performed this song live once, in the early phase of the Bob Dylan and The Band 1974 Tour.

Prospect Park, Minneapolis | Prospect Park is a historic neighborhood within the University community of the U.S. city of Minneapolis, Minnesota.  The area is bounded by the Mississippi River to the south, the City of Saint Paul, Minnesota to the east, the Burlington Northern railroad yard to the north, and the Stadium Village commercial district of the University of Minnesota to the west.  The neighborhood is composed of several districts which include the East River Road area.  The 1913 Prospect Park Water Tower is a landmark and neighborhood icon.

Shelter from the Storm | "Shelter from the Storm" is a song by Bob Dylan, released on his 15th studio album, "Blood on the Tracks", in 1975.  Along with "Tangled Up in Blue", "Shelter from the Storm" was one of two songs from "Blood on the Tracks" to be re-released on the 2000 compilation "The Essential Bob Dylan".  The song also appears on two live albums by Bob Dylan — "Hard Rain" (from a May 1976 performance) and "At Budokan" (recorded in February 1978).  A first take of the song, from the same recording session that produced the album track, is included on "The Best of Bob Dylan, Vol.  1" (1997).

All I Really Want to Do | "All I Really Want to Do" is a song written by Bob Dylan and featured on his Tom Wilson-produced 1964 album, "Another Side of Bob Dylan" ("see" 1964 in music).  It is arguably one of the most popular songs that Dylan wrote in the period immediately after he abandoned topical songwriting.  Within a year of its release on "Another Side of Bob Dylan", it had also become one of Dylan's most familiar songs to pop and rock audiences, due to hit cover versions by Cher and the Byrds.

The 30th Anniversary Concert Celebration | The 30th Anniversary Concert Celebration is a live double-album release in recognition of Bob Dylan's 30 years as a recording artist.  Recorded on October 16, 1992, at Madison Square Garden in New York City, it captures most of the concert, which featured many artists performing classic Dylan songs, before ending with three songs from Dylan himself.

All Along the Watchtower | "All Along the Watchtower" is a song written and recorded by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan.  The song initially appeared on his 1967 album "John Wesley Harding", and it has been included on most of Dylan's subsequent greatest hits compilations.  Since the late 1970s, he has performed it in concert more than any of his other songs.  Different versions appear on four of Dylan's live albums.
hop1_titles: As I Went Out One Morning
All I Really Want to Do
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Prospect Park Water Tower — [1]: "The Prospect Park Water Tower"
- "All Along the Watchtower" — [1]: "is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song \"All Along the Watchtower,\""
- "All Along the Watchtower" — [7]: "performed it in concert more than any of his other songs."

Ambiguities:
- Inspiration status is reported as a "rumor" in [1] ("is rumored to be the inspiration..."), i.e., not asserted as confirmed.

Bridge / missing fact:
- None — the passages together name "All Along the Watchtower" as both the song said to be inspired by the tower [1] and as the song Dylan has performed live most often [7]. Final answer: "All Along the Watchtower".

Retrieval direction:
- Page: "All Along the Watchtower"
- Page: "Prospect Park Water Tower"
- Page: "Bob Dylan" (for comprehensive performance statistics)
- Query: "Bob Dylan most performed songs list"
No deferral — passages supply the needed facts above.
response.hop2_query: All Along the Watchtower Prospect Park Water Tower
hop2_docs: Prospect Park Water Tower | The Prospect Park Water Tower, sometimes referred to as the "Witch's Hat Water Tower" or just "The Tower", is a historic water tower in the Prospect Park neighborhood of Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.  It was built in 1913 on Tower Hill Park, a hilltop park established in 1906.  The water tower has become the neighborhood's architectural mascot for its singular design by Frederick William Cappelen.  The tower is rumored to be the inspiration for Bob Dylan's song "All Along the Watchtower," as the tower was clearly visible from Dylan's home in nearby Dinkytown.

Prospect Park, Minneapolis | Prospect Park is a historic neighborhood within the University community of the U.S. city of Minneapolis, Minnesota.  The area is bounded by the Mississippi River to the south, the City of Saint Paul, Minnesota to the east, the Burlington Northern railroad yard to the north, and the Stadium Village commercial district of the University of Minnesota to the west.  The neighborhood is composed of several districts which include the East River Road area.  The 1913 Prospect Park Water Tower is a landmark and neighborhood icon.

Bruton Dovecote | The Bruton Dovecote is a limestone tower that was built between the 15th and 17th century in Bruton in the English county of Somerset.  The structure was once used as a dovecote, and may have been a watchtower or prospect tower prior to this.  It is a Grade II* listed building and scheduled monument.

The Watchtower | The Watchtower Announcing Jehovah's Kingdom is an illustrated religious magazine, published monthly by Jehovah's Witnesses via the Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society of Pennsylvania.  Along with its companion magazine, "Awake! ", Jehovah's Witnesses distribute "The Watchtower—Public Edition" in their door-to-door ministry.
All Along the Watchtower (TV series) | All Along the Watchtower is a British sitcom that aired on BBC One in 1999, about an RAF site in Scotland, it was written by Pete Sinclair and Trevelyan Evans.

Chicago Water Tower | The Chicago Water Tower is a contributing property and landmark in the Old Chicago Water Tower District.  It is located at 806 North Michigan Avenue along the Magnificent Mile shopping district in the Near North Side community area of Chicago, Illinois in a small park, the Jane M. Byrne Plaza.  The tower was constructed to house a large water pump, intended to draw water from Lake Michigan.  It is the second-oldest water tower in the United States, after the Louisville Water Tower in Louisville, Kentucky.

Tilehurst Water Tower | Tilehurst Water Tower, also sometimes known as Park Lane Water Tower, is a distinctive water tower in Tilehurst, a suburb of the English town of Reading.  It is a prominent landmark in the vicinity and, located on the Tilehurst ridge line, can be seen from a considerable distance, especially when approaching Reading from the west along the M4 motorway.
